title: Out Of Commission

jokes.text: Well, I guess my memory's okay then.
jokes.setup: Tony asks about a fight he can't remember, gets told it was over in one punch
jokes.punchline: Tony concludes his memory is fine since there's nothing to remember
jokes.explanation: Smart misdirection - we expect Tony to be concerned about memory loss, but he finds comfort in having nothing to remember. Good character logic.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So if it was over in one punch, there's nothing to remember.

jokes.text: I usually don't unless they've been especially nice.
jokes.setup: Tony asks if he should tip the paramedics who carried him
jokes.punchline: Tony treats emergency medical care like restaurant service
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony logic - approaching serious situations with casual restaurant etiquette mindset.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Should I tip the paramedics?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I mean, they carried me out on a stretcher, they loaded me in the ambulance... that's gotta be worth at least 15 percent.

jokes.text: Man, I told you this was the wrong locker room.
jokes.setup: Opponent checks on Tony after knocking him out, showing class
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's lost and looking for his own locker room
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and payoff - reframes the entire 'classy gesture' as accidental confusion.

dialogue.speaker: Opponent
dialogue.line: Hey, you okay? I didn't mean to hit you that hard.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, I'm fine.

dialogue.speaker: Opponent
dialogue.line: Listen, I feel terrible. Can I get you anything? Water? Towel?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm good. Thanks for asking though.

dialogue.speaker: Opponent
dialogue.line: Which way is the locker room? I've been wandering around here for ten minutes trying to find mine.

jokes.text: Who else you know rakes in five dollars a second?
jokes.setup: Tony only gets $20 for getting beaten up in a boxing match
jokes.punchline: Tony finds the positive spin - high per-second earnings
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Tony's eternal optimism finds the silver lining in getting destroyed for almost no money.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Twenty bucks? That's all I got for getting beaten up for three rounds?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wait, wait... let me do the math here. Three rounds, three minutes each... that's nine minutes total.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Twenty dollars divided by nine minutes... that's two dollars and twenty-two cents a minute!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Hey, that's not bad! I make more per minute getting my face rearranged than I do driving a cab!

jokes.text: Well, it depends what you mean by knockout. I mean, uh, sometimes I'm just taking a rest, and the ref is counting real fast...
jokes.setup: Doctor asks how many times Tony's been knocked out
jokes.punchline: Tony redefines knockouts as 'taking a rest' with fast-counting refs
jokes.explanation: Excellent character logic - Tony's self-preservation through creative redefinition. The 'fast-counting ref' detail sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: How many times have you been knocked out?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Knocked out? I don't know, doc. I lost count after about... well, a lot of times.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: But I don't think of it as being knocked out. I think of it as taking a rest.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: The ref just counts real fast, so by the time I'm ready to get up, he's already at ten.

jokes.text: He leads with his right.
jokes.setup: Doctor asked what's wrong with Tony's body
jokes.punchline: Gives boxing technique advice instead of medical assessment
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection - doctor momentarily thinks like a boxing fan instead of medical professional.

dialogue.speaker: Tony Banta
dialogue.line: Doc, what's wrong with me? I got this pain in my shoulder.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Webster
dialogue.line: Well, let me see. You're dropping your right shoulder when you throw a punch. Keep it up, protect your chin.

dialogue.speaker: Tony Banta
dialogue.line: Doc, I'm not asking for boxing advice. I'm asking what's medically wrong with me!

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Webster
dialogue.line: That IS medically wrong with you, Tony. You've got a boxer's injury.

jokes.text: All you did was ask me my name. Yeah, but you gave me the wrong answer. But once you told me what it was, I recognized it.
jokes.setup: Doctor didn't like Tony's reaction to post-knockout exam
jokes.punchline: Tony forgot his own name but recognized it when told
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony logic - finding the positive in obvious brain damage. The escalation from wrong name to recognition is beautifully crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Webster
dialogue.line: What's your name?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I don't know.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Webster
dialogue.line: Tony Banta.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's it!

jokes.text: A lot of guys yelled at me to get out of the ring. Some of them might have been doctors.
jokes.setup: Asked if any doctor advised him to quit boxing
jokes.punchline: Tony uncertain if the people telling him to quit were doctors or just concerned spectators
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Tony's so punch-drunk he can't distinguish medical advice from crowd reactions.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm not sure if they were doctors or just concerned spectators.

jokes.text: I'd like a shot at him.
jokes.setup: Doctor tells story of brain-damaged fighter who can't fight anymore
jokes.punchline: Tony wants to fight the brain-damaged guy
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Tony response - completely missing the cautionary point and seeing opportunity. Dark and brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wait a minute, wait a minute. This guy can't fight anymore?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'll fight him.

jokes.text: I been knocked out more times than I can remember.
jokes.setup: Tony arguing that getting knocked out isn't a big deal
jokes.punchline: Claims he's been knocked out more times than he can remember - proving the doctor's point
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic self-defeat - Tony accidentally proves the doctor's concern about brain damage while arguing against it.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I've been knocked out more times than I can remember.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: That proves my point.

jokes.text: Tony, you're not helping your case.
jokes.setup: Tony just said he's been knocked out more times than he can remember
jokes.punchline: Doctor's dry observation about Tony undermining his own argument
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man response to Tony's self-defeating argument.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Webster
dialogue.line: Well, that certainly undermines your argument.

jokes.text: ♪ Happy birthday, crazy lady ♪
jokes.setup: Forced to sing happy birthday to his mother
jokes.punchline: Calls her 'crazy lady' instead of using her name
jokes.explanation: Pure Louie - even singing happy birthday becomes an insult. Character-specific humor.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Happy birthday to you, happy birthday to you, happy birthday dear crazy lady, happy birthday to you.

jokes.text: I just get hit on by a crummier class of creeps.
jokes.setup: Elaine dresses frumpy to avoid being hit on while driving
jokes.punchline: The strategy backfires by attracting worse quality harassers
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about unintended consequences. The phrase 'crummier class of creeps' is well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So I'm driving in the cab, right? And I'm getting hit on constantly. Every red light, every stop sign. So I figure, I'll dress down. You know, make myself look less attractive.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I put on this old sweatshirt, no makeup, hair in a mess. I look terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: And you know what happens? It works! No more harassment. But now I'm getting a different kind of attention.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Now the guys who stop by are way worse. Like, I'm getting hit on by guys who are missing teeth, guys with visible stains on their shirts, guys who smell like they bathed in cologne to cover up something worse.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I went from being too attractive to being the perfect target for people I wouldn't have given a second look to before.

jokes.text: Hey, foxy lady.
jokes.setup: Elaine just explained how dressing frumpy attracts worse men
jokes.punchline: Immediately proven right by a creepy catcall
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing to prove Elaine's point, but the content is fairly predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Random creep
dialogue.line: Hey baby, where are you going looking like that?

jokes.text: Hey, foxy lady.
jokes.setup: Elaine just explained about attracting crummier creeps
jokes.punchline: Immediate demonstration of her point
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing - immediately validates Elaine's point. Simple but effective callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Creep
dialogue.line: Hey baby, what's your sign?

jokes.text: No, but he sure was a good listener.
jokes.setup: Jim delivers professional taxi company spiel to wrong number caller
jokes.punchline: Evaluates the confused caller as a 'good listener'
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim logic - finding positive qualities in completely inappropriate interactions.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Thank you for calling the Sunshine Cab Company. If you'd like a cab, press 1. If you'd like to make a complaint, press 2.

dialogue.speaker: Caller
dialogue.line: [confused silence/unclear response]

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You're a good listener.

jokes.text: Oh, the glamour's part of it.
jokes.setup: Elaine describing boxing as standing before bums and creeps getting your head bashed
jokes.punchline: Tony sincerely calling this glamorous
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery. Tony's complete sincerity about the 'glamour' of getting beaten up is beautifully delusional.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Glamorous!

jokes.text: Oh, the glamour's part of it.
jokes.setup: Bobby describes boxing as getting beat up in front of bums and creeps
jokes.punchline: Tony treats the negative description as highlighting boxing's appeal
jokes.explanation: Good Tony moment - his romantic view of boxing persists despite brutal reality description.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? That's the best part!

jokes.text: you know, like death.
jokes.setup: Bobby says he's not really a cab driver, just waiting for something better
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's waiting for death
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark Bobby humor - escalates from job dissatisfaction to existential despair with perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm not really a cab driver. I'm just waiting for something better.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm waiting for death.

jokes.text: I love Chinese restaurants.
jokes.setup: Tony says he's going to 'tie one on' (get drunk)
jokes.punchline: Jim misunderstands and thinks they're going for Chinese food
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim misunderstanding - innocent interpretation of drinking slang.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm going to tie one on.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh great! I love Chinese food!

jokes.text: I made a fortune betting against you.
jokes.setup: Tony expects sympathy from Louie about losing boxing license
jokes.punchline: Louie reveals he profited by betting Tony would lose
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - turns Tony's tragedy into his personal loss. Completely in character to profit from employee's failure.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Louie, I lost my boxing license. I need your sympathy.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Sympathy? Tony, I made two hundred bucks betting you'd lose.

jokes.text: She's starting not to look like one.
jokes.setup: Louie's mother has 'female problems'
jokes.punchline: The problem is she's becoming masculine-looking
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie crude humor about his mother. Character-appropriate but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: My mother's got female problems.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The problem is she's becoming masculine-looking.

jokes.text: Only the playwright and anyone trapped in the theater.
jokes.setup: Elaine says when Bobby acts, no one gets brutalized unlike boxing
jokes.punchline: Bobby admits his acting brutalizes playwrights and audiences
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-deprecating humor - Bobby acknowledges his terrible acting while maintaining the metaphor structure.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Well, yeah, but when I act, nobody gets brutalized.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's true. Unlike boxing.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Well, actually, I brutalize playwrights and audiences.

jokes.text: Years.
jokes.setup: Asked how long he's been waiting to use the playwright/theater joke
jokes.punchline: Admits to years of preparation for this moment
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up joke - Louie's patient setup for insulting Bobby's acting.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Years? I've been waiting years to use that joke!

jokes.text: Must have got it in a fire sale. That and the suit he's wearing. Does he work in there, or is he just on display?
jokes.setup: Lou-Lou bought Tony's contract
jokes.punchline: Three-part escalation: fire sale, bad suit, human display piece
jokes.explanation: Good escalating insult sequence - Louie attacks both the business deal and Lou-Lou's appearance.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what I'm gonna do with you, Tony? I'm gonna have a fire sale. Everything must go!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: A fire sale? Louie, come on—

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Then I'm gonna buy you the worst suit in New York and make you wear it every day.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Louie, please—

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And finally, I'm gonna put you in a glass case in the window of Macy's as a human display piece.

jokes.text: That explains his buenos dias yesterday.
jokes.setup: Learning Tony will fight as 'Kid Rodriguez'
jokes.punchline: Connects earlier Spanish greeting to new identity
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that retroactively explains Tony's behavior. Good structural writing.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Hey Tony, 'Kid Rodriguez'? That's a great name for a fighter.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, you like it?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Sure. Say, whatever happened to 'Hola'?

jokes.text: And if that's what he wants, there are easier ways of getting there.
jokes.setup: Describing boxing's brain damage effects
jokes.punchline: Implies there are simpler ways to become brain damaged
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about brain damage alternatives. Well-structured buildup and payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You don't have to be a boxer to get brain damage. There's easier ways.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You could just drive a cab in New York.

jokes.text: You've been called 'sir' for the last time.
jokes.setup: Calling his mother about her operation
jokes.punchline: References earlier joke about mother looking masculine
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to earlier setup about mother's 'female problems.' Good structural payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Ma, how'd the operation go? ...Yeah? ...Uh huh... Oh that's wonderful, Ma. The doctor said you're gonna be fine? ...Oh, that's terrific news, Ma. I'm so relieved. ...What's that, Ma? ...The doctor said what? ...He said you looked like you could've been a linebacker? Ma, that's not funny. That's not funny at all!

jokes.text: And, besides, the bull doesn't stand a chance.
jokes.setup: Jim condemning boxing as savage blood sport
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's thinking of bullfighting instead
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim confusion - passionate speech about wrong sport. Good character-specific absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Boxing is a savage blood sport. It's barbaric, it's brutal, it's everything that's wrong with civilization.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm talking about bullfighting.

jokes.text: I'll pay you $1,000 if you don't hurt my face. Give me five. All right! I mean dollars. Okay.
jokes.setup: Tony offering money to avoid facial damage
jokes.punchline: Opponent thinking he means high-five, clarification makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy beat with the misunderstanding. Tony's desperation comes through in the clarification.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Here, take fifty bucks.

dialogue.speaker: Opponent
dialogue.line: What's this for?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So you don't rearrange my face.

dialogue.speaker: Opponent
dialogue.line: Oh! You want to high-five!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No, I want to give you money so you don't hit me.

dialogue.speaker: Opponent
dialogue.line: Now I'm definitely gonna hit you.

jokes.text: I mean dollars.
jokes.setup: Opponent offers $1000 not to hurt his face, Tony gives him five
jokes.punchline: Clarifies he meant dollars, not a high-five
jokes.explanation: Simple misunderstanding gag. Functional but not particularly inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Tony's opponent
dialogue.line: I'll give you a thousand dollars if you don't hurt my face.

dialogue.speaker: Tony Banta
dialogue.line: Deal!

dialogue.speaker: Tony's opponent
dialogue.line: Wait, I meant dollars, not a high-five.

jokes.text: if you put on your shoes last, it won't be so hard pulling your pants on over them.
jokes.setup: Jim offering fashion advice to boxer Tony
jokes.punchline: Reveals Tony put his shoes on before his pants
jokes.explanation: Good visual absurdity - implies Tony is so confused he's dressing incorrectly. Jim's helpful tone makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Tony, you've got to think about the overall look. Shoes before pants? That's not a fashion statement, that's a cry for help.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? I always do it this way.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That explains a lot.

jokes.text: Just the one with reality.
jokes.setup: Asked how many fights Alex has been in
jokes.punchline: Alex only fights with reality/life itself
jokes.explanation: Excellent metaphorical punchline - reframes life struggles as boxing matches. Great economy and wit.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: How many fights have you been in?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, I don't fight with people. I only fight with reality.

jokes.text: Me llamo Kid Rodriguez. Kid Rodriguez, me llamo. Hola.
jokes.setup: Tony asked if he's acting weird from being punchy
jokes.punchline: Immediately starts speaking Spanish in character
jokes.explanation: Perfect physical comedy - Tony proves Alex's point about acting weird while denying it. Great character commitment.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Weird? No, no, no. I'm not acting weird. I'm perfectly fine. Everything is normal.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: ¡Ay, Dios mío! ¿Qué está pasando aquí? Yo soy un hombre muy importante!

jokes.text: these two scrappy, Latino contenders
jokes.setup: Boxing announcer introducing fighters
jokes.punchline: Describes Tony as Latino when audience knows he's Italian-American
jokes.explanation: Good situational irony - official announcement validates Tony's disguise for audience who knows the truth.

dialogue.speaker: Ring announcer
dialogue.line: In this corner, weighing 185 pounds, Tony Banta!

dialogue.speaker: Ring announcer
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, he's a real Latin fighter!

jokes.text: You lost weight.
jokes.setup: Announcer calls his weight at 162 pounds
jokes.punchline: Tony notices he's lost weight mid-announcement
jokes.explanation: Mild humor - Tony distracted by personal details during formal introduction.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: 162 pounds

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. That can't be right. I was 165 this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I lost three pounds since I got here?

jokes.text: You think I'm quitting with a record like that?
jokes.setup: Announces Tony's record as 23-8 (fake good record)
jokes.punchline: Tony acts proud of record that isn't actually his
jokes.explanation: Good irony - Tony suddenly confident with borrowed record, forgetting it's not really his achievement.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Twenty-three and eight? That's my record?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I didn't know I was that good!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wait, whose record is that?

jokes.text: La cucaracha, man.
jokes.setup: Opponent wishes Tony good luck in Spanish
jokes.punchline: Tony responds with random Spanish song title
jokes.explanation: Shows Tony's limited Spanish vocabulary - resorts to song title when trying to communicate.

dialogue.speaker: Opponent
dialogue.line: Buena suerte.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Gracias. 'Besame Mucho'.

jokes.text: el stopo
jokes.setup: Tony trying to quit fight in Spanish
jokes.punchline: Makes up Spanish by adding 'el' to English words
jokes.explanation: Excellent fake Spanish - shows Tony's commitment to character even while quitting. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm trying to quit, okay? I'm telling him I want to stop fighting.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So I says to him, 'No más el fighting!'

jokes.text: I'm going to get together all the money I made in boxing all these years, buy myself a new suit.
jokes.setup: Tony planning what to do with his boxing earnings
jokes.punchline: After years of boxing, he can only afford one suit
jokes.explanation: Good closing joke - summarizes Tony's financial situation and boxing career failure in one line.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: After years of boxing, he can only afford one suit

tmdb_overview: After Tony is beaten badly in his most recent boxing match, the state takes his boxing license away.
insight: Taxi's deadpan sweet spot: 40 jokes in 24 minutes without breaking character once.
